summary: Anglo-Saxon poet, author of Caedmon's Hymn — the oldest extant piece of Old English poetry. An illiterate cow-herd at Whitby Abbey under St. Hilda who left a banquet because he 'did not know how to sing,' then received divine inspiration in a dream ('Sing me creation'). The resulting nine-line (eighteen half-line) poem praises God as creator, describing 'heaven as a roof' and 'middle-earth' for the 'children of earth.' The poem survives only in copies of the Venerable Bede's Ecclesiastical History. The Dream of the Rood, in which the cross narrates the Crucifixion, is sometimes attributed to Caedmon. A major codex of Old English literature is named for him, though he almost certainly wrote none of its contents.

comprehensive_summary:
Caedmon is an Anglo-Saxon poet, author of Caedmon's Hymn, the oldest extant piece of Old English poetry. According to the Venerable Bede's Ecclesiastical History of the English People, Caedmon was an illiterate cow-herd at Whitby Abbey under the Abbess Hild (St. Hilda). He left a banquet because he 'did not know how to sing,' and a figure in a dream told him 'Sing me creation.' He then composed the Hymn, a nine-line (eighteen half-line) alliterative poem praising God as creator, opening 'Now we ought to praise the Guardian of the heavenly kingdom.' The poem describes God creating 'heaven as a roof' and 'middle-earth' for the 'children of earth,' and refers to 'the work of the Glory-Father.' It survives only in copies of Bede's History.

The poem uses alliterative verse, which replaced rhyme in Old English poetry. The use of the word 'scop' in the poem has led to debates about whether Caedmon is analogizing divine creation to poetry. A song sung in Hrothgar's hall in Beowulf may be connected to Caedmon's Hymn.

The Dream of the Rood, an Old English poem preserved in the Vercelli Codex, is sometimes attributed to Caedmon (or Cynewulf). In it, the cross narrates the story of Christ's crucifixion from its own perspective, covered in gems. Part of the poem is also carved on the eighth-century Ruthwell Cross in Scotland.

A fourth major codex of Old English literature (alongside the Exeter Book, Vercelli Codex, and Nowell Codex containing Beowulf) is named for Caedmon, though he almost certainly wrote none of its contents. Caedmon was a contemporary of Aldhelm, who wrote the Aenigmata (Latin riddles). Sean Anthony argued the story of Caedmon's life was based on accounts of Muhammad by Ibn Ishaq. Alasdair Gray's The Book of Prefaces begins with analysis of Caedmon's Genesis.
